On May 10, 1857, they shoot their officers, free their imprisoned comrades, and ride through the night toward Delhi — toward the only living symbol of Mughal authority — seeking a legitimacy that only an old, cautious emperor can give.

episodes.title: The Explosion of May
episodes.summary: The sepoys crowd into the Hall of Audience. They ask Zafar to place his hands on their heads. He hesitates. Then he does.
episodes.description: May 11, 1857. The Hall of Audience in the Red Fort. Sepoys who arrived from Meerut overnight — grimy, exhausted, still carrying weapons — crowd forward and ask the emperor to place his hand on their heads: a blessing that transforms a mutiny into an imperial revolution. Zafar hesitates. His caution is legendary. Then he chooses. His hand descends. Half a mile away, Lieutenant George Willoughby detonates the great magazine, sending a massive explosion through the city's center that kills hundreds. In a single morning: an imperial blessing and a military catastrophe, synchronized. British survivors flee. The Ridge north of the city becomes a British camp — but instead of falling as expected, Delhi fills with rebel forces from across northern India. The British besieging force finds itself effectively besieged. The Red Fort fills with soldiers who have no unified command. And in the palace at the center of the storm, the old poet-king has placed his hand on something he cannot take back.

episodes.title: Siege, Stalemate, and the Turn of the Tide
episodes.summary: For three months, the British hold the Ridge. Delhi holds them back. Then the reinforcements arrive.
episodes.description: Three months. British forces hold the Ridge in conditions of extraordinary suffering: cholera, dysentery, heat, flies, constant sniping, and the knowledge that every day without decisive action means another day of rebel reinforcement. Inside Delhi, the rebellion fragments. Factions within the city dispute strategy, authority, and resources. Zafar manages his court — still holding ceremonies, still writing poetry, still trying to manage fractious military commanders who will not listen to an old man who never wanted this war. On August 1, Eid al-Adha. The city is in the grip of a potential communal explosion. Zafar's careful precautions prevent it. Outside the city, British reinforcements finally arrive. Artillery is positioned. Breaching batteries are established. The balance begins to shift — not dramatically, but with the slow accumulation of weight. By early September, the siege is breaking open. The final assault on Delhi's walls is days away. The city that has survived three months is about to fall in days.

episodes.title: Fall, Retribution, and Exile
episodes.summary: Zafar is found in a tomb, brought back to Delhi in a palanquin — no longer a sovereign, not even a prisoner. Something smaller.
episodes.description: September 1857. British forces breach the walls and fight through Delhi in days of brutal street combat. Nicholson, the legendary British officer whose reckless courage defines the assault, is mortally wounded and dies days later. Zafar flees to Humayun's Tomb and is found there, brought back to Delhi in a palanquin — not as sovereign, not as ceremonial prisoner, but as something smaller, something already half-erased. William Hodson, the British cavalry officer who captures him, executes three Mughal princes in cold blood the same day, on a Delhi street, in front of a crowd. Zafar is tried, convicted of rebellion, and exiled to Rangoon in 1858, where he dies in 1862. His grave is made level with the turf so that it will disappear. It does not disappear. From the ruins of 1857, two Muslim futures emerge: one looking westward toward modern education at Aligarh, one looking inward toward Islamic revival at Deoband. The poet-emperor is gone. His shadow remains.

collections.title: The Struggle for Pakistan
collections.author: Ayesha Jalal
collections.summary: Pakistan was born as a homeland for Muslim minorities who feared Hindu majority rule. Within a decade, its elected governments had been replaced by a military that would never fully give power back. Ayesha Jalal's landmark history follows four episodes of Pakistan's founding paradoxes, military coups, Cold War bargains, and the unfinished democratic transition that still defines — and haunts — one of the world's most consequential states.

episodes.title: Founding Paradoxes: Minority Politics to Partition
episodes.summary: Two assassinations at the same park, fifty-six years apart. Pakistan's instability is written into its founding.
episodes.description: Liaquat Bagh, Rawalpindi. On October 16, 1951, Pakistan's first prime minister Liaquat Ali Khan is shot dead at a public meeting. The assassin is killed immediately; the crime scene is contaminated; the inquiry produces no verdict. Fifty-six years later, on December 27, 2007, Benazir Bhutto is assassinated at the same park. The crime scene is washed clean within hours. Again, no verdict. The parallel is not coincidence — it is structure. The episode rewinds to Jinnah's decades-long argument that Muslims needed political protection in a Hindu-majority India, the Lahore Resolution of 1940, the 1946 election that hardened positions beyond compromise, and the creation of Pakistan in August 1947: truncated, divided, born in refugee upheaval and communal violence, immediately embroiled in the Kashmir crisis. A homeland imagined as protection for the vulnerable arrives in the world already contested, already governed by institutions that will not yield to democratic pressure, already carrying contradictions that will take seventy years to name.

episodes.title: Barracks State: Militarization and the Road to 1971
episodes.summary: The Pakistani army wins the 1970 election result and then refuses to honor it. What follows destroys half the country.
episodes.description: March 25, 1971. Flames spread across Dhaka's horizon as the Pakistani army launches its assault on Bengal. A country founded to protect Muslim self-determination is destroying its own largest city. The episode traces how this catastrophe was not inevitable but chosen: after Liaquat's assassination, bureaucrats and the military fill the vacuum. Ayub Khan's 1958 coup installs controlled democracy — civilian facade, military architecture. The 1965 war with India exposes brittleness; unrest spreads. In the 1970 election, Sheikh Mujibur Rahman's Awami League wins an overwhelming national mandate. The military junta refuses to honor it. When negotiations fail — not because compromise was impossible, but because the junta chose force over accommodation — the army moves on Dhaka. Within months, East Pakistan is a war zone. Ten million refugees flood into India. By December, India's military intervention produces Pakistani surrender. Bangladesh is born. West Pakistan has lost more than half its population. No apology has ever been offered.

episodes.title: Populism, Islamization, and Managed Democracy
episodes.summary: Bhutto believes he has hours left to negotiate. Zia launches his coup at midnight.
episodes.description: Midnight, July 5, 1977. Zulfikar Ali Bhutto, still performing confidence, believes he has hours to negotiate with the opposition. He does not. General Muhammad Zia-ul-Haq has already launched the coup. Bhutto is arrested, tried for conspiracy to murder, and hanged in April 1979. He is fifty-one years old. Zia rules for eleven years, using the Afghan jihad as a source of American money and weapons, and Islam as a governing instrument: Islamization of law, mandatory religious practice in public institutions, the systematic use of blasphemy provisions against minorities and dissidents. When Zia dies in a plane crash in 1988, Pakistan briefly reopens. Benazir Bhutto and Nawaz Sharif cycle through power in the 1990s — each dismissed on corruption charges, each returned by election, each operating in a system where the army decides where civilian authority ends. The machinery built to manage democracy has learned to cage it instead. Every time civilians enter office, the question is not whether the army will intervene, but when.

episodes.title: Geostrategic Endgames: Terror and Transition
episodes.summary: Pakistan simultaneously hands over Arab al-Qaeda operatives to the Americans — and airlifts Taliban commanders to safety.
episodes.description: November 21, 2001. The United States halts airstrikes on Kunduz to allow Pakistani aircraft to evacuate more than a thousand fighters — including Pakistani intelligence officers and senior al-Qaeda figures — from the besieged Taliban city. The pause is not surrender. It is extraction. President Bush praises Musharraf's exemplary role in the war on terror. That is the bargain at its most naked: Pakistan is indispensable to Washington, and impossible to trust. The episode follows Musharraf's impossible navigation — appeasing Washington while sheltering Taliban infrastructure, fighting terrorist networks while maintaining strategic assets — and the slow accumulation of democratic pressure beneath the military surface. The Abbottabad raid kills bin Laden. Musharraf falls. Civilian governments survive, weakly. In 2013, for the first time in Pakistan's history, one elected civilian government transfers power to another. It is a small thing. It is also, in the context of Pakistani history, an extraordinary one.

collections.title: The Great War for Civilisation
collections.author: Robert Fisk
collections.summary: Nine episodes follow Robert Fisk’s reporting across Afghanistan, Iran-Iraq, Lebanon, Palestine, Algeria, the Gulf War, sanctions, 9/11, and the invasion of Iraq. It is a portrait of war’s human cost and the cycles that keep returning.

episodes.title: Afghanistan, Empire, and the First Encounter with Bin Laden
episodes.summary: The Soviet war in Afghanistan seeds a jihad that will outlive its sponsors.
episodes.description: Robert Fisk enters Afghanistan as the Soviet war grinds on, a conflict where empires test their will on a brutal landscape. This episode moves from the refugee camps of Pakistan to the mountain front, following the mujahideen’s guerrilla war and the foreign money that keeps it alive. Stinger missiles and the CIA-ISI pipeline turn ideology into logistics. We meet the emerging Arab volunteers and a young Osama bin Laden, still treated as a useful ally rather than a global threat. The war is narrated through civilians caught between Soviet bombardment, warlord politics, and Western cameras hungry for a simple story. Fisk’s reporting complicates the narrative: the victors of one jihad may become the architects of the next. As the Soviets begin to withdraw, Pakistan and the United States declare success. The question that hangs over the last scenes is what happens when a war ends without a settlement. Peace will be deferred, not delivered.

episodes.title: Revolution and Fire: Iran, Iraq, and a War Without Limits
episodes.summary: Revolution and invasion turn Iran and Iraq into a furnace of chemical war.
episodes.description: The Iranian revolution topples a king and electrifies the region, but the new Islamic Republic is immediately tested by war. The hostage crisis deepens the isolation of Tehran while emboldening its revolutionary rhetoric. This episode moves from Tehran’s upheaval to Saddam Hussein’s invasion, a conflict that devours a decade and a generation. Fisk reports from front lines where teenagers charge minefields and cities trade missile strikes. Chemical weapons appear, and the world looks away; oil politics matter more than law. Millions are displaced. The war redraws sectarian and nationalist identities, hardens dictatorship in Baghdad, and leaves Iran both proud and scarred. Western governments arm Iraq while condemning it in public, and the Gulf monarchies bankroll a stalemate that never feels stable. By the time the ceasefire arrives, no one has truly won. The question is what kind of Middle East this furnace has forged, and who will pay for it next.

episodes.title: Private Memory, Public Catastrophe
episodes.summary: Lebanon’s civil war and the 1982 invasion expose a city’s wounds and a reporter’s memory.
episodes.description: Lebanon’s civil war turns a cosmopolitan country into a maze of checkpoints and disappearances. Fisk reports from Beirut as militias, foreign armies, and intelligence services carve the city into fear. This episode follows the Israeli invasion of 1982, the siege of West Beirut, and the massacres at Sabra and Shatila, where civilians are abandoned to their killers. The PLO’s expulsion and the collapse of the multinational force show how outsiders promise peace and then leave. It is also a story of memory: of journalists trying to bear witness, of families searching for the missing, of a war that never ends cleanly. We see how propaganda hardens every side and how truth becomes another casualty. The violence is intimate and bureaucratic at once, carried out by men who are neighbors one day and executioners the next. By the end, Lebanon is a warning: a country can survive on paper while its society is ripped apart.

episodes.title: Palestine: From Mandate Promises to Intifada Ruins
episodes.summary: From mandate promises to intifada, Palestine lives under occupation and contested memory.
episodes.description: Palestine sits at the center of the region’s unresolved argument. Fisk traces the story from the British mandate and competing promises to the wars of 1948 and 1967, when displacement and occupation become permanent facts. The episode follows the growth of settlements, the daily humiliations of military rule, and the emergence of the PLO as both symbol and target. The exile in Lebanon, the 1982 invasion, and the grinding life under occupation sharpen the sense of irreversibility. The first intifada breaks the illusion that the conflict can be managed, and a generation of Palestinian youths takes to the streets with stones and strikes. Israeli politics hardens in response, even as secret negotiations hint at a possible peace. We see how language—terrorism, security, liberation—becomes another battlefield. By the end, the Oslo moment arrives with hope and deep suspicion. The episode asks whether any agreement can survive the realities it refuses to name.

episodes.title: Algeria's Dirty War
episodes.summary: Algeria’s cancelled election ignites a dirty war of disappearances and massacres.
episodes.description: Algeria’s postcolonial story erupts in the 1990s when an election threatens military rule. The episode follows the rise of the Islamic Salvation Front, the army’s cancellation of the vote, and the descent into a dirty war that blurs every line between state and insurgent. Villages are massacred, disappearances become routine, and the security services operate in the shadows while claiming to defend the republic. Fisk visits survivors who describe night raids and mass graves, and he questions the official story that blames only the Islamists. Fisk reports on the fear that grips ordinary people who no longer know who is killing whom. The conflict is sold abroad as a fight against extremism, yet it devours civilians first. By the time the violence begins to burn out, the country is exhausted and its political life hollowed. The silence afterward feels uneasy. The episode asks whether stability bought through terror can ever produce legitimacy.

episodes.title: Desert Storm and the Betrayed Aftermath
episodes.summary: Desert Storm’s swift victory leaves uprisings crushed and sanctions entrenched.
episodes.description: Saddam’s invasion of Kuwait triggers a U.S.-led war that seems to promise a quick, clean victory. Fisk reports on the air campaign’s destruction, the highway of death, and the language of precision that hides civilian cost. Scud strikes on Israel, coalition diplomacy, and UN sanctions become the new architecture of containment. It is fragile. The ceasefire is a pause, not peace. The episode follows the uprising that follows the ceasefire: Kurds in the north and Shiites in the south rise up, believing the coalition will back them. Instead, Saddam’s forces crush the revolts while the world watches. The result is a regime left in power, a population left exposed, and a region that learns the price of American promises. The war ends without settlement, and Iraq becomes a sealed box of sanctions, smuggling, and suffering. Fisk’s reporting asks whether the conflict truly ended in 1991, or simply laid the ground for a much larger reckoning.

episodes.title: Slow Violence: Sanctions, Toxic Legacies, and the Arms Bazaar
episodes.summary: Sanctions, toxic legacies, and the arms trade reveal a slower, bureaucratic violence.
episodes.description: After the Gulf War, Iraq is strangled by sanctions and a sanctions-busting economy. Fisk reports on hospitals without medicine, children’s malnutrition, and the quiet arithmetic of death that never makes front pages. The episode explores the politics of embargo: how Washington and London insist the pressure is humane while the regime adapts and civilians pay. The Oil-for-Food program becomes a symbol of corruption, with the UN caught between relief and complicity. We also enter the arms bazaar—dealers, governments, and covert networks that profit from perpetual conflict, from the Gulf to the Balkans. The toxic legacy of war lingers in contaminated water and unexploded ordnance, turning peace into another hazard. This is the violence of bureaucracy and economics, slower but no less deadly. Few admit the cost. By the end, Iraq is weakened but not transformed, and the question is whether the world has created a catastrophe it can no longer walk away from.

episodes.title: Levantine Ghosts, 9/11, and the Road to Iraq
episodes.summary: 9/11 recasts the region and pushes the United States toward Iraq.
episodes.description: The late 1990s feel like a long warning. Fisk moves through the Levant as old wars leave new ghosts—Hizbullah’s rise, Syria’s grip on Lebanon, and the unfinished business of Palestine. Then 9/11 arrives, and the region is recast as the theater of a global war. Meanwhile, Iraq remains under sanctions, and Saudi bases and Israeli-Palestinian violence fuel radical recruitment. The region hears drums of war. It is not yet unstoppable. This episode tracks how the attacks are interpreted in the Middle East and in Washington, and how the hunt for al‑Qaeda morphs into an argument for invading Iraq. Intelligence is stretched, history is simplified, and dissent is dismissed as disloyalty. Fisk reports from Afghanistan and the Gulf as the rhetoric of liberation grows louder than the evidence. The episode ends on the eve of invasion, when the logic of preemption has become policy and the last chances to stop it are slipping away.

episodes.title: The Sack of Baghdad and the Age of Occupation
episodes.summary: The fall of Baghdad opens an occupation defined by looting, insurgency, and sectarian war.
episodes.description: Baghdad falls quickly, but the real war begins afterward. Fisk reports from a city stripped of order: ministries looted, museums emptied, and ordinary Iraqis left to improvise survival under occupation. The episode follows the dissolution of the Iraqi army, the vacuum it creates, and the rapid rise of insurgent and sectarian violence. De‑Baathification and the disbanded police force turn resistance into a mass movement, and Fallujah becomes a symbol. Checkpoints become targets, militias take neighborhoods, and the promise of liberation curdles into humiliation. Abu Ghraib’s images crystallize the collapse of moral authority, while U.S. officials insist a new Iraq is being built. Fisk listens to families, doctors, and shopkeepers who measure policy in injuries and funerals. By the time elections arrive, the country is fractured and the occupation’s legitimacy is gone. The episode ends with a question that echoes back to Afghanistan: what does victory mean when the state itself is broken?
